coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of porous materials of high mechanical strength with graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Dongdong
Qin, Zihao
Liu, Yongfeng
Di, Duolong
Wang, Hao
Liu, Yi
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: A series of porous materials with good mechanical and fluorescence properties enhanced by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared using surfactant reverse micelle swelling method with acrylamide (AAM) and 1-vinylimidazole (VIM) as monomers, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as crosslinking agent and azobisisobutyronitrile(AIBN) as initiator. Effects of additional amount of GQDs on the pore structure, fluorescence property, zeta potential and mechanical properties of the porous materials were systematically investigated. The surface area and pore volume increased as introduction of GQDs, while average pore width increased at first and then decreased with the amount of GQDs increasing, verifying the successful preparation of the porous materials. The fluorescence intensity changed to certain degree by the introduction of GQDs, which might resulted from photoelectron transfer between the GQDs and polymer matrices. The mechanical strength of the porous materials was greatly enhanced with introduction of GQDs due to the increase in storage modulus and permeability. The present work will shed light on the preparation of porous materials with high mechanical strength and fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: porous material
mechanical strength
graphene quantum dot
fluorescence
rheological property
surfactant reverse micelle swelling method

Rheological properties of the porous materials
Mechanical stability of the porous materials

SEM images of the prepared porous materials VAEG-0 (a), VAEG-1 (b), VAEG-2 (c), and VAEG-3 (d).
Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of the prepared porous materials.
Fluorescence spectra of the prepared porous materials.
The relationship between storage modulus and angular frequency.
The relationship between pressure and flow rate.
Physical properties of the prepared porous materials.
The permeability parameters of the prepared porous materials.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of porous materials of high mechanical strength with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Degradable and Flame-Retardant Polymeric Materials, College of Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, National Engineering Laboratory of Eco-Friendly Polymeric Materials, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Degradable and Flame-Retardant Polymeric Materials
State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering
National Engineering Laboratory of Eco-Friendly Polymeric Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Resource Chemical and New Material, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Qingdao 266100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Resource Chemical and New Material

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strong fluorescence, excellent mechanical and permeable organic porous polymers VAEG-n were prepared and showed good application potential as chromatographic packing.

A series of porous materials with good mechanical and fluorescence properties enhanced by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared using surfactant reverse micelle swelling method with acrylamide (AAM) and 1-vinylimidazole (VIM) as monomers, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as crosslinking agent and azobisisobutyronitrile(AIBN) as initiator. Effects of additional amount of GQDs on the pore structure, fluorescence property, zeta potential and mechanical properties of the porous materials were systematically investigated. The surface area and pore volume increased as introduction of GQDs, while average pore width increased at first and then decreased with the amount of GQDs increasing, verifying the successful preparation of the porous materials. The fluorescence intensity changed to certain degree by the introduction of GQDs, which might resulted from photoelectron transfer between the GQDs and polymer matrices. The mechanical strength of the porous materials was greatly enhanced with introduction of GQDs due to the increase in storage modulus and permeability. The present work will shed light on the preparation of porous materials with high mechanical strength and fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Porous organic polymers are classical materials employed widely as adsorption materials [1,2], catalysts [3–5] and chromatographic stationary phases [6,7] due to their special properties, such as easy preparation, controlled porosity and diverse surface chemistry [8–12]. However, they usually suffer from swelling as exposed to different organic mobile phases, leading to weak mechanical stability, when they were used as chromatographic packing [13]. Therefore, it is a prerequisite and challenge task to enhance the mechanical stability of porous organic polymers before they are used as chromatographic packings.

Carbon based nanostructures, including MWCNTs, graphene, carbon black, and their composites with metal or metal oxide nanoparticles are potential candidates as additives for improving the performances of the polymer matrices such as electroconductivity [14–16], adsorptivity [17,18] and catalytic activity [19].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an important graphene nanomaterial, are nano-sized carbon-based quantum dots with single-layer graphene cores and connected chemical groups, which exhibited similar physical and chemical properties comparing to graphene and graphene oxide(GO) [20]. KOH activated GQDs displayed very strong fluorescent and capacitance performances [21]. Graphene, as a two-dimensional material, composed of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, exhibits unique thermal and electrical properties [22–24]. Particularly, graphene still has excellent mechanical performance and 1 TPa modulus of elasticity and 125GPa of fracture strength of graphene have been reported [25]. One possible pathway to exploit the extraordinary properties of graphene is to incorporate and disperse graphene in different matrices, like polymers, ceramics. Graphene has been widely used in the field of polymer reinforcement so far. GO with ultimate stress at about 60GPa and Young’s modulus about 250GPa, is considered as mechanically strong and rigid two-dimensional material [26,27]. It was reported that the tensile strength of agar with 5% PVA was 38.31±5.35MPa with an elongation of (14.37±4.46)%. But when 2wt% GO was incorporated into the film, the tensile stress rose to 83.69±3.62MPa with elongation of (7.72±3.21)%, which indicated the mechanical properties were greatly enhanced after the introduction of GO due to high interfacial bonding between GO and agar [28]. The elongation was increased by 89% from 28.6% to 54.0% and the tensile strength was increased by 87% from 17.5MPa to 32.7MPa when only 1.0wt% of GO sheets was added into the PA/PPO blends due to strong interactions between GO sheets and PA and PPO phases and low interfacial tension [29]. Graphene and functionalized graphene still showed good compatibility with particular polymer matrix [30]. In this case, it can be deduced that the mechanical properties of porous organic polymers can be enhanced through the introduction of GQDs with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups due to the interactions between functional groups of GQDs and polymer chains.

The objective of the present study was to exploit the potential of GQDs to improve the mechanical strength of porous organic polymers prepared with N-vinyl imidazole and acrylamide as monomers and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as crosslinker. Subsequently, effect of additional amount of GQDs in the porous organic polymers on the pore structure, fluorescence properties and mechanical strength was systematically investigated.

Citric acid (CA) of analytical grade was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Biotopped dialysis bags were purchased from Beijing Aodada Technology Co., Ltd. Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) (Tianjin Yuefeng Chemical Co., Ltd.) of analytical grade was recrystallized from methanol before use. Hexadecane (HD) of analytical grade, was used as a hydrophobic additive to prevent the monomer from diffusing into the aqueous phase [31]. Span 80 (Laiyang Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd., Shandong, China) was of chemically pure. The polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) of analytical grade (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) was used as a stabilizer. Hydroquinone (HQ) of analytical grade (Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) was applied to prevent secondary nucleation in the aqueous phase. Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) of biochemical grade(J&K Scientific Ltd., China) was used to associate with PVA in solution and form a complex at the interface for reducing the interfacial tension and stabilizung the droplets [32]. Acrylamide (AAM) and 1-vinylimidazole (VIM) of analytical grade (Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) were used as monomers. Toluene of analytical grade was bought from Li’anlong Bohua (Tianjin) Pharmaceutical Chemical Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) of analytical grade (Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used as a crosslinking agent. Na2SO4 of analytical grade (Sichuan Chemical Co., Ltd.) was used to adjust the electrolyte concentration of the water phase. All other reagents are analytical grade and are used without further purification.

GQDs were obtained by direct pyrolysis of CA according to the reference [33]. 2g of CA was placed in a 250mL round bottom flask with an oil bath and heated to 473K. After about 4–6minutes, CA began to melt into transparent solution. Subsequently, the color of the liquid changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then gradually turned to be orange in 30minutes. After the reaction finished and the flask was cooled, 100mL NaOH solution of 10mg/mL was added under vigorously stirring. After the mixture was neutralized to pH 7, the GQDs solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut off of 1000 D for 3days and then GQDs were obtained.

The preparation procedure of the porous materialswas similar to previous report method [34]. A typetical preparation procedure is as follows: In the first step, the stabilizer (PVA, 1.0g), the surface active agent (SDS, 0.015g), the electrolyte (Na2SO4, 0.02g), the inhibitor (HQ, 0.01g) and certain amount of GQDs (0, 0.024, 0.047 and 0.141g, which was respectively equal to 0, 0.6, 1.2 and 3.6wt% of the total weight of the monomers and crosslinking agent) were successively dissolved in deionized water(100mL) in a four-necked flask and used as a continuous phase (aqueous phase). And then, a mixture of monomers (AAM, 1.5g and VIM, 1.4mL), crosslinking agent (EGDMA, 1.0mL), toluene (5mL), HD (0.2g), Span 80 (1.6g) and initiator AIBN (0.12g) was dissolved as a disperse phase (monomer phase). The monomer phase was stirred violently at speed of 4000rpm for 10min to ascertain the oil phase was mixed thoroughly. Finally, the mixed monomer phase was charged into the continuous phase drop by drop in the flask with an anchor stirrer, condenser and nitrogen inlet nozzle. After purging with nitrogen for one hour, the temperature was raised to 348K to carry out the reaction for 20hours under nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting materials were washed four times with boiling distilled water and ethanol in turn. Subsequently the porous materials were further extracted with acetone for 24hours to remove porogens and impurities and then dried in vacuum at room temperature. The porous materials were denoted as VAEG-n where n was respectively 0, 1, 2 and 3 according to additional amount of GQDs.

The specific surface area and average pore size of VAEG-n were measured by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corp., Norcross, USA) through nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm curves obtained at 77K. Prior to the BET surface area measurement, the VAEG-n materials were degassed at 373K for 24hours on the degassing port of the analyzer. Infrared spectra of the samples were obtained in the wavenumber range of 400cm−1 to 4000cm−1 by potassium bromide pallet on a Nicolet 8700 FT-IT spectrometer(Nicolet, USA). Raman spectra were measured by a RFS100/S Fourier transform Raman spectrometer (Brucker, Germany). UV-visible absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 35 UV-visible spectrometer. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the products were measured using EMPYREANX-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Holland). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a DETLASERLES TGA 7 thermogravimetric analyzer(Perkin-Elmer, USA) with nitrogen atmosphere, the temperature was set from room temperature to 1073K and the heating rate was kept at 283K/min. Fluorescence spectra were measured on a F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer(Hitachi, Japan). The porous material fixed on the cover glass was used for morphological and fluorescent measurements by a LSM710META fluorescence confocal laser scanning scanning microscopy (Carlzeiss, Germany). The steady-state shear rheological properties of the suspension of the porous materials were measured by a Physica MCR301 rheometer(Anton Paar, Austria). The surface morphology of the materials was observed by a JEM-6700F cold field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, JPEL, Japan) and a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (TEM, USA). Zeta potential was determined with a Nano-ZS 3600 zetasizer instrument (Malvern, British).

Rheological properties were measured as follows: 1.0g VAEG-n was added to 25mL ethanol aqueous solution (vdistilledwater:vethanol =1:1) and violently stirred at 11000rpm for 10min. The shear rate was in the range of 0.1–100 1/s. The angular frequency was in the range of 0.1–100rad/s. The test temperature was set at 298K.

The mechanical stability of VAEG-n was determined by connecting a column (150 mm×4.6mm, id. 5μm) filled with VAEG-n of 1.0g to a semi-preparative liquid chromatography and the backpressure was obtained by pumping the mobile phase (ACN/H2O, 50:50, v/v).

To investigate which functional group was involved in the reaction, the FTIR spectra of VAEG-n were studied and the results were shown in Fig. 1 . The absorption band at 3440cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH of GQDs and NH2 of amide. The bands at 2956 and 1445cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of C-H, respectively. The peaks at 1562, 1390 and 666cm−1 were ascribed to CN stretching, CN stretching and CN bending vibrations of the imidazole ring [35], respectively. After introduction of GQDs, the absorption band at 1562cm−1 decreased greatly. The peak at 1730cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of CO in carboxyl group of GQDs and ester group of the crosslinker, respectively. The band at 1678cm−1 was related to CO in amide group and CN in imidazole ring [36], respectively. The band at 1154cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C of the crosslinker. It could be observed that there was slight difference in the absorption peaks after the introduction of GQDs.

In spite of high background due to strong fluorescent emission and low content of GQDs, Raman spectra of the GQDs porous polymers with excitation wavelength of 532nm (Fig. S1 in the supplementary information) showed obvious GQDs characteristic scattering peaks at 1730–1750cm−1(G peaks) and at 2740–2750cm−1 (2D peaks). The blue shifts relative to conveniently prepared graphene sheets resulted from reduced size of GQDs and existence of oxygen-containing groups at edges of GQDs [37,38]. The results further proved formation of GQDs incorporated polymers. XRD pattern of each polymer composite (Fig. S2) showed a weak and wide peak at 2θ=16–19° suggesting non-crystalline characteristics, the weak characteristic diffraction peak at∼23° of graphene [39] was not observed due to too low content of GQDs.

The TGA was performed to determine the thermal stability of the prepared materials and provide evidence of introduction of GQDs into the porous materials, and the TGA and DTA curves were shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4. There was an obvious weightloss process from 573K to 723K, which may be ascribed to the degradation of the structural units and the main chain of the polymer [40], which indicated the materials possessed relatively high thermostability. A slight weight decrease between 323K and 473K may be related to removal of the water molecules adsorbed onto the porous materials [41]. There was a little difference from each other of the porous materials VAEG-n, which may be resulted from different additional amount of GQDs, indicating the interaction force between GQDs and the polymer was relatively weak. There was a distinct difference from each other in DTA curves. Furthermore, the zeta potentials of VAEG-0, VAEG-1, VAEG-2 and VAEG-3 were determined to be 13.4mV, 16.6mV, 16.8mV and 18.6mV, respectively, indicating the introduction of GQDs could change the physic-chemical properties of the prepared porous materials. The information mentioned above suggested that the VAEG-n materials were successfully prepared.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of VAEG-n were shown in Fig. 2 . These isotherms belonged to type IV isotherms according to IUPAC classification, with a step capillary condensation in the relative pressure of 0.1–0.9. The adsorption amount for nitrogen under the same relative pressure was in the order of VAEG–3> VAEG–1> VAEG–2> VAEG-0, which was in line with the order of BET surface area. Furthermore, the pore volume (Table 1 ) was in same trend as BET surface area, while average pore width (Table 1) was in the order of VAEG–1> VAEG–2> VAEG-3, indicating small pore width led to high surface area. Obviously, VAEG-3 possessed the biggest specific area and pore volume. The information mentioned above suggested that the pore structure changed with the introduction of GQDs into the porous materials. The increase in BET surface area and pore volume might enhance the adsorption performance for several analytes. Small hysteresis loop could be observed in the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm curves of all the prepared porous materials, indicating mesopores were present in the materials [42,43]. At the relative pressure of higher than 0.9, the amount of nitrogen adsorbed increased sharply, possibly suggesting the existence of macropores in the materials [44]. The pore width distribution was also studied and the results were given in Fig. 3 . It could be seen that the pores in the VAEG-n materials consisted of micropores, mesopores and macropores, and the contribution of pores to the pore volume was larger after introduction of GQDs, suggesting GQDs might result in change in pore structure. The information further verified that the pore volume was enhanced due to the introduction of GQDs. The above results showed that GQDs took part in formation of the pore structures. In the formation and growth procedure of the porous composites, GQDs served as crosslinking agent and porogen simultaneously. So with addition amount of GQDs increasing, the pore width and pore volume increased too. But when the addition amount of GQDs was too high, partial GQDs would enter the pore so that the pore width decreased.

Morphological images of VAEG-n were illustrated in Fig. 4 . For VAEG-0, the surface was loose and the material was consisted of small aggregates. After introduction of GQDs, the surface turned to be more compact and dense, which may be because GQDs provided more crosslinking sites for the formation of hydrogen bonding and covalent bonding, and thus it was favorable for the construction of network structure. The formation of macropores might be ascribed to the interspace between the aggregates. The results indicated that the morphology of the porous materials was strongly affected by the introduction of GQDs, which was consistent with the results of N2 adsorption-desorption experiments. The results also further verified the successful preparation of the porous materials with GQDs. EDS results (Fig. S5–Fig. S8) showed C, N and O elements were uniformly distributed in the composite polymer matrices. On the whole, with increasing of addition amount of GQDs, the content of C increased and the contents of N and O decreased, which indicated a similar change trends to the mechanical and permeable properties of the composites. TEM and SAED results showed all of the four materials were amorphous (Fig. S9).

Laser confocal scanning microscopy was also employed to visualize the morphological change of the porous materials with introduction of GQDs (Fig. 5 ). The prepared porous materials VAEG-n with an excitation wavelength of 405nm showed an intense blue fluorescence [45]. However, there was a little difference in the blue fluorescent from each other. The fluorescence of VAEG-0 and VAEG-1 was weaker than that of VAEG-2 and VAEG-3 and in GQDs composites evident hollow structure as observed. The results suggested that the fluorescence would be enhanced by the introduction of certain amount of GQDs. Blue emission, or enriched electron density of the π structure to a certain degree was mainly ascribed to the OH based hybrid structure [46]. The green fluorescence would be observed with excitation wavelength of 488nm, and the change trend in strength of the fluorescence was similar to that of blue fluorescence. It was reported that the green emission mainly resulted from carboxyl and amide groups in GQDs and the organic polymer [46]. Under the condition of excitation, OH groups tended to be converted to carbonyl/carboxyl groups, and the change would improve the surface state emission(green emission) of the GQDs and VAEG-n [47]. The results were in line with those of fluorescence spectra analysis. The fluorescence observed from the porous materials would broad their application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of the porous materials
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral analysis results of GQDs were shown in Figs. S10-12 of supporting information, which verified GQDs were successfully prepared. The fluorescence spectra of VAEG-n were shown in Fig. 6 . It could be seen that one strong emission peak appeared in the fluorescence spectrum of each porous materials when it was excited at 257nm. The fluorescence intensity was in the order of VAEG–3> VAEG–1≈VAEG–0> VAEG-2 at emission wavelength of 393nm. The their bsorption peaks resulted from n-π* and surface state transitions of nitrogen or oxygen-containing groups, and the hybrid structure of the partial carbon skeleton and edge groups [48]. Due to the presence of amide, ester and imidazole ring, the prepared porous materials VAEG-n could emit fluorescence. In recent years, it has been attempted to explore GQDs for the development of sensing systems based on the changes of fluorescence intensity due to the photo induced electron transfer [49]. The changes of fluorescence intensity of the porous materials with introduction of GQDs might resulted from the quenching or enhancement effect caused by photoelectron transfer [50,51]. Hence, the prepared fluorescent porous materials were expected to possess a potential application in optical sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological properties of the porous materials
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the mechanical performance of the prepared porous materials, rheological properties were investigated in ethanol aqueous solution. Fig. 7 shows the changes of storage modulus with increasing angular frequency. It can be seen that the storage modulus of the porous materials remained constant and overlapped with each other until angular frequency of 10rad/s. When the angular frequency exceeded to 10rad/s, the storage modulus speedily rose except VAEG-0 and the gap between the storage modulus also gradually increased with the freuency increasing. The storage modulus changed in the order of VAEG–3> VAEG–2> VAEG–1> VAEG-0, indicating introduction of GQDs into the porous materials could enhance storage modulus and strengthened the interaction between GQDs and polymer matrix [29]. As a result, the mechanical strength was improved too. In addition, the values of storage modulus were higher than those of loss modulus (the data were not shown), which was an indicator of solid-like relaxation behavior, indicating the prepared porous materials behaved as a viscoelastic solid under this circumstance [52,53]. The relationship between angular frequency (ω) and complex viscosity (ƞ*) can give the information concerning the solid-like mechanical response [54]. A linear relationship between logƞ* and logω with a slope of −1 related to a perfectly elastic solid [55,56]. It was found that in the frequency range of 3.16–100rad/s, the slopes of the lines of VAEG-0, VAEG-1, VAEG-2, and VAEG-3 were 0.91, 0.99, 0.93 and 0.56, respectively, indicating introduction of GQDs was indeed favorable for the formation of the network structure. The formation of hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of GQDs and amide, ester and imidazole of the organic polymer chains promoted the construction of network structure. However, redundant hydrogen bonding may cause entanglement of organic polymer chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical stability of the porous materials
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanical stability of VAEG-n was evaluated through the back pressure measurements at different flow rates by connecting the column to a semi-preparative liquid chromatography pump, and the relationship between the flow rate and the back pressure was shown in Fig. 8 . To be seen clearly, the relationship between the flow rate and the back pressure of each porous material was separatively shown in Fig. S13 too. As the flow rate increased from 0.01mL/min to 0.03mL/min, the back pressure linearly increased for VAEG-0 and VAEG-2, while VAEG-1, the back pressure increased linearly with flow rate increased from 0.01 to 0.04mL/min. It is notable that the back pressure of VAEG-3 showed a ider linear range from 0.01mL/min to 0.8mL/min. The results indicated that VAEG-n possessed good mechanical stability at a certain flow rate and the mechanical stability of VAEG-3 was the best among the four porous materials. The permeability is an important factor for evaluating the resistance of a packed column (or empty tube) to fluid flow [57]. It was calculated by Darcy's law [57]:
B n = FƞL/(πr 2ΔP)
where F, η, L, r and ΔP are the flow rate and viscosity of the mobile phase (ACN/H2O=50:50, ƞ =0.81 cP), the effective length and the inner diameter of the column, and the column pressure drop, respectively. The permeability of VAEG-n was shown in Table 2 . It was found that the permeability was in the order of VAEG–3> VAEG–1> VAEG–2> VAEG-0, indicating that the introduction of GQDs could effectively improve permeability of the porous materials and VAEG-3 showed the highest permeability.

A series of porous materials (VAEG-n) with excellent mechanical and fluorescence properties were prepared by employing surfactant reverse micelle swelling method. Introduction of GQDs into the polymer matrices could effectively improve the fluorescent properties and mechanical performances of the resulting porous materials. As addition amount of GQDs increased, the specific area and pore volume enhanced. The GQDs composite porous materials demonstrated also high mechanical and excellent permeability and could be used as one of candidates of chromatographic packing to separate certain analytes. Present work provides a general strategy to prepare porous materials with high mechanical strength by introduction of GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the joint support by Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province of China (1501RJZA011), West Light Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21605150, 21544013 and 21665024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2017.10.087.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
